title: Aqua Security Trivy Supply Chain Attack CVE-2026-33634 (CVSS 9.4): TeamPCP Injects CanisterWorm Credential-Stealing Malware into 76 Version Tags — Blockchain-Based C2 Infrastructure Propagates to 141+ npm Packages
affected_products: All downstream npm packages infected by CanisterWorm

sources.name: Arctic Wolf - TeamPCP Supply Chain Attack Campaign
Help Net Security - CVE-2026-33634 Exploited in the Wild
PenLigent Security - Mutable Tags and the Trivy Supply Chain Compromise
Aqua Security Blog - Trivy Supply Chain Attack: What You Need to Know
Aikido Security - TeamPCP Deploys CanisterWorm via npm Trivy Compromise
CISA - Adds CVE-2026-33634 to Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog
Docker Blog - Trivy Supply Chain Compromise: What Docker Hub Users Should Know
OffSeq Threat Radar - CVE-2026-33634 Embedded Malicious Code
Windows Forum - CISA Adds Trivy CVE-2026-33634 to KEV

timeline.event: Initial breach: Attackers exploit GitHub Actions misconfiguration in Aqua Security's Trivy repository, extracting a privileged access token
Aqua Security discovers the February breach and begins credential rotation — but rotation is non-atomic, leaving residual access for attackers

TeamPCP exploits retained credentials to force-push malicious commits to 76 of 77 trivy-action version tags and all 7 setup-trivy tags; publishes malicious Trivy v0.69.4 to all distribution channels (GitHub Releases, Docker Hub, GHCR, Amazon ECR Public, Debian/RPM repos)
Malicious Trivy v0.69.4 binary and images detected and removed from registries (~4 hour window for binary, ~12 hours for GitHub Actions)
CanisterWorm discovered propagating to 141+ npm packages using ICP blockchain canister as C2 infrastructure — first documented case of blockchain-based malware C2
CISA adds CVE-2026-33634 to Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog with federal remediation deadline of April 8, 2026
Arctic Wolf publishes research revealing broader campaign targeting Checkmarx KICS, LiteLLM, and additional open-source security projects

simple_summary.what_happened: Aqua Security's Trivy — the world's most widely used open-source container vulnerability scanner — was secretly compromised by a hacking group called TeamPCP. The attackers used stolen administrator credentials to inject credential-stealing malware into Trivy's official GitHub releases, Docker images, and automated CI/CD actions (used in thousands of software pipelines). Any organization that ran Trivy between March 19–20, 2026 may have had all their cloud credentials, API keys, SSH keys, and other secrets silently stolen. The malware also spread to 141+ npm software packages using blockchain technology for its command-and-control infrastructure — a first of its kind technique.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Any organization using Trivy for container or infrastructure security scanning is potentially affected, particularly those using: Trivy v0.69.4 (any distribution channel), aquasecurity/trivy-action GitHub Action (versions 0.0.1 through 0.34.2), aquasecurity/setup-trivy GitHub Action (any version before 0.2.6), or any npm package infected by CanisterWorm (141+ packages). This affects cloud-native teams, DevOps engineers, and security teams across thousands of organizations globally.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Attackers (TeamPCP) stole privileged GitHub credentials from Aqua Security in February 2026 by exploiting a GitHub Actions misconfiguration. Step 2: They waited while Aqua Security did an incomplete credential rotation, retaining backdoor access. Step 3: On March 19, they used the stolen credentials to replace the official Trivy binary and GitHub Actions with malware-infected versions across all distribution channels simultaneously. Step 4: When victims' CI/CD pipelines ran Trivy scans, the malware secretly harvested all accessible secrets — cloud credentials, SSH keys, API tokens, database passwords — from pipeline memory and the filesystem. Step 5: Stolen secrets were encrypted and exfiltrated to attacker servers, with a fallback of creating a hidden GitHub repository ('tpcp-docs') on the victim's account. Step 6: On developer machines, the malware installed a persistent backdoor that polls a blockchain smart contract every 5 minutes for new attack commands. Step 7: Using stolen npm tokens, CanisterWorm propagated automatically to 141+ npm packages, infecting downstream users who installed those packages.
what_you_should_do: Immediately check if your organization used Trivy v0.69.4, trivy-action (any version before 0.35.0), or setup-trivy (any version before 0.2.6) on or around March 19–20, 2026

Treat ALL secrets accessible to any affected CI/CD pipeline as fully compromised — rotate GitHub tokens, AWS/GCP/Azure credentials, SSH keys, Kubernetes service account tokens, Docker registry credentials, and database passwords simultaneously
Check GitHub Actions workflow logs from March 19–20 for unexpected processes, environment variable dumps, or repository creation events (look for 'tpcp-docs' repository pattern)
Update to safe versions: Trivy v0.69.3 or v0.69.5+, trivy-action v0.35.0+, setup-trivy v0.2.6+
Pin all GitHub Actions to immutable commit SHAs rather than mutable version tags to prevent future tag-hijacking attacks
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: This attack is exceptionally serious for three reasons. First, Trivy is trusted by thousands of organizations specifically to find security vulnerabilities — attackers weaponized that trust to steal the keys to cloud infrastructure. Second, the CanisterWorm's use of blockchain (ICP canister) for command-and-control infrastructure is unprecedented and cannot be taken down through conventional hosting provider takedowns. Third, the attack had a cascading effect: stolen npm tokens were used to propagate the malware to 141+ additional packages, meaning organizations that never used Trivy directly may still be compromised through transitive dependencies. The CISA KEV designation confirms active exploitation.

tactics.technique_name: Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Supply Chain
tactics.description: TeamPCP compromised Trivy's official distribution channels (GitHub Releases, Docker Hub, GHCR, Amazon ECR, Debian/RPM repos) by using stolen credentials to inject malicious code into legitimate builds
On developer machines, a persistent systemd user service was installed to execute malicious payloads delivered via ICP canister on a 5-minute polling interval

tactics.technique_name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Registry Run Keys / Startup Folder
tactics.description: Systemd user service (sysmon.py) installed for persistent execution, surviving reboots and operating invisibly within normal system service framework
Malware dumped Runner.Worker and Runner.Listener process memory via /proc/<pid>/mem to extract CI/CD runtime secrets and tokens
Systematically scanned 50+ filesystem paths for SSH keys, cloud credentials, kubeconfig files, .git/config, and other stored secrets
Attacker spoofed legitimate GitHub commit authors (rauchg for actions/checkout, DmitriyLewen for aquasecurity) to make malicious commits appear authentic
Stolen credentials encrypted with AES-256 + RSA-4096 and exfiltrated via HTTPS to C2 domain (scan[.]aquasecurtiy[.]org), with fallback to GitHub repository creation ('tpcp-docs')
Destructive wiper variant detected systems with Asia/Tehran timezone or fa_IR locale and executed rm -rf / to destroy data — targeted destructive component alongside credential theft

detection_guidance.summary: Detection focuses on identifying whether your environment executed the compromised Trivy artifacts between March 19-20, 2026, monitoring for credential exfiltration indicators, and detecting CanisterWorm activity in npm dependencies. Primary signals include unexpected process memory access in CI/CD logs, outbound connections to the typosquatted C2 domain, and suspicious GitHub repository creation.

log_sources.description: Check workflow run logs from March 19-20, 2026 for any jobs using trivy-action or setup-trivy. Look for unexpected environment variable dumps, process listing, or repository creation events
Scan for outbound HTTPS connections to 'scan.aquasecurtiy.org' (typosquatter domain) and any unusual ICP blockchain network traffic
Monitor for unexpected repository creation events — particularly any repository named 'tpcp-docs' — which indicates the malware's fallback exfiltration mechanism was triggered
Look for unusual process executions spawned from Trivy scan steps, /proc/<pid>/mem file access patterns, or filesystem scanning operations outside expected scan scope
On developer machines, check for unexpected new systemd user services, particularly 'sysmon.py' or similar Python-based services created around March 19-21, 2026
Run npm audit on all projects to detect CanisterWorm-infected packages in dependency trees; compare package hashes against known-good versions

what_to_look_for.description: Typosquatted domain (note: 'aquasecurtiy' not 'aquasecurity') used as primary exfiltration endpoint for stolen credentials

what_to_look_for.indicator: GitHub Repository 'tpcp-docs' created on victim account
what_to_look_for.description: Malware creates a hidden repository named 'tpcp-docs' as a fallback data exfiltration channel when the primary C2 is unreachable
Any CI/CD job that executed Trivy version 0.69.4 between March 19-20, 2026 should be treated as compromised

what_to_look_for.indicator: trivy-action versions 0.0.1-0.34.2 used in GitHub Actions
what_to_look_for.description: Any GitHub Actions workflow referencing aquasecurity/trivy-action@v0.0.1 through v0.34.2 during the compromise window
Unusual blockchain network traffic to Internet Computer Protocol canister endpoints indicates CanisterWorm C2 polling activity
what_to_look_for.log_field: url.domain CONTAINS '.ic0.app' OR url.domain CONTAINS 'icp0.io' AND process.name='python'

what_to_look_for.description: CanisterWorm installs a persistent Python-based systemd user service disguised as system monitoring

immediate_actions.description: Check GitHub Actions workflow logs, CI/CD pipeline histories, and container orchestration logs to determine if your organization ran Trivy v0.69.4, trivy-action (pre-v0.35.0), or setup-trivy (pre-v0.2.6) during the compromise window

If any exposure is confirmed, treat every secret accessible to affected pipelines as compromised. Rotate GitHub tokens, AWS/GCP/Azure credentials, SSH keys, Kubernetes tokens, Docker credentials, and database passwords in a single coordinated operation — non-atomic rotation (as Aqua Security did) allows attackers to retain access

Update trivy-action to v0.35.0+, setup-trivy to v0.2.6+, and Trivy binary to v0.69.3 (stable) or v0.69.5+. Pin all GitHub Actions to immutable commit SHAs instead of version tags to prevent future tag-hijacking
immediate_actions.command:
# Update trivy-action in workflows:
# uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@v0.35.0
# Pin to SHA: uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@<full-commit-sha>

immediate_actions.action: Check for CanisterWorm in npm Dependencies
immediate_actions.description: Run npm audit and dependency analysis to identify any of the 141+ npm packages infected by CanisterWorm. Compare installed package versions and checksums against known-good versions from before March 19, 2026
immediate_actions.command: npm audit --audit-level=moderate && npm list --all | grep -f known-compromised-packages.txt

immediate_actions.action: Search for Persistent Backdoor on Developer Machines
immediate_actions.description: On any developer machine that may have pulled malicious artifacts, check for the CanisterWorm systemd user service and remove it. Also check for unauthorized GitHub repository 'tpcp-docs' on organization accounts
immediate_actions.command: systemctl --user list-units --type=service | grep -i sysmon; ls ~/.config/systemd/user/ | grep -v snap
